instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
How to Claim for a Mis-sold Life Insurance Policy
Meta-description: Think you may have been mis-sold a life insurance policy? Here are the next steps.
Keywords: ULIP plan, mis-selling, insurance mis-selling
Introduction
Any provider selling insurance products has the duty to act in the best interests of their customer. This means that the product should fit your circumstances and enhance your situation rather than take away from it. Ensuring that you are given the ins and outs of a policy when it is introduced to you is one way to keep up the camaraderie between insurance providers and customers. If an insurer fails to intentionally serve in the best interests of their customer, they may be guilty of mis-selling their insurance policy.
What is insurance mis-selling?
When an insurance policy is claimed to be other than what it is, not broken down in its completeness to a customer, or sold to the client outside of this target audience intentionally by an agent or financial party, this is called mis-selling of insurance. Insurance mis-selling is, unfortunately, rather common for ULIP plans but it is also seen in the case of life insurance. A common example of mis-selling life insurance is when an agent may persuade an individual to opt for a life insurance policy while the individual has no dependents. Most forms of mis-selling insurance, if not all, are illegal, but catching them as soon as possible is an essential action to be taken successfully.
How can life insurance be mis-sold?
Examples of life insurance being mis-sold include:
Being told or being persuaded by a financial party or an agent that you have to take out your life insurance cover prematurely as there are no other options. Not only is this untrue, as there are always other options, but helping your coverage grow is also essential to building up a savings corpus that can help your dependents in case of an untimely death. Taking out your cover prematurely, especially when the situation does not demand this, may not help you reap all of the benefits of your policy.
Not being explained the terms and conditions of your policy in detail. There are multiple clauses to any kind of insurance policy and knowing when and how you can claim your life insurance is valuable before you buy your insurance. If your insurer or bank has skipped the terms and conditions by not sharing them with you, or not briefing you on them, you can consider this to be a form of mis-selling.
Being sold an insurance policy that you cannot claim. This is applicable to the aforementioned example of when life insurance is sold to an individual with no dependents. There is no beneficiary to claim the policy which means that your money will go and stay with the financial parties that have fraudulently given you the policy. This is perhaps the worst way in which life insurance can be mis-sold, and customers can seek legal action against the corrupt party if they are unfortunate victims.
Being given incorrect advice on the best type of product or service for you, or being sold more than you require. Let's assume that an insurer informed you about ‘whole of life’ insurance —which is a full coverage and relatively more expensive form of life cover, but they did not offer a ‘term’ cover as another option for you. Whole life insurance will offer a payout no matter what age the policyholder perishes at while term cover will pay out its lump sum if the loss occurs within a certain duration that is fixed at the time the policy is bought. If your provider failed to elaborate that other cheaper options are available to you, especially when those options are more suitable to your needs, you have been mis-sold life insurance.
Can I claim compensation for life insurance mis-selling?
Yes, if you have been mis-sold life insurance or even a ULIP plan (which comprises life coverage) for that matter, you have the grounds to claim compensation for the same.
What can I do about mis-sold life insurance?
If you believe you have mis-sold a policy, revisit and scrutinise your paperwork carefully and keep an eye out for any added costs/lack of services/contradictory information for your insurance that you may have unknowingly signed. Once you are confident you were mis-sold a life insurance start by:
Gathering an inventory of reasons that explain how you have been mis-sold a life insurance policy.
Compiling together all evidence that can support your claims including things like emails or text messages between you and your agent/policy provider, pamphlets that market the policy in a deceptive manner, false claims or reviews on the insurer’s website, and more.
Keep all these documents at your disposal since you will likely be complaining to the company or insurer that wrongfully sold you the policy.
Conclusion
Insurance mis-selling is malpractice that can be nipped in the bud if policyholders are scrupulous about what they are buying. Check out the range of life insurance options at BALIC to find a policy that fits your needs.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale.
Source:
https://www.moneysupermarket.com/life-insurance/mis-sold/
